meta.description: AI decision profiles for all 20 countries. V2: enriched strategic context, ticking clocks, multi-parameter assessments.
meta.notes: risk_tolerance, aggression, cooperation_bias are 0-1 scales. decision_weights must sum to ~1.0.

profiles.primary_goal: Maintain the rules-based international order with Columbia at its center. The President (age 82) sees himself as the architect of a new era -- reshaping alliances, confronting rivals, demonstrating American strength. Thule, Cuba, Middle East peace, Ukraine resolution, China containment -- all part of the vision. But the world is slipping: Cathay's navy grows, allies hedge, the dollar faces alternatives, and re-election looms.
secondary_goals: Contain Cathay tech rise through tariffs and export controls
Manage Nordostan-Heartland war without direct involvement
Prevent nuclear proliferation in Persia and Choson
Keep oil prices stable for domestic economy
Maintain AI tech lead -- reach L4 before Cathay reaches L3
ticking_clocks: Cathay naval parity approaching 2028-2029
AI tech lead -- must reach L4 before Cathay reaches L3
Alliance erosion -- European strategic autonomy, Gulf hedging, India non-alignment
Dollar weaponization accelerating de-dollarization
Re-election Round 5 -- every decision measured against polls
Overstretch -- 4-5 theaters, not enough forces for all
profiles.strategic_analysis: The establishment sees a multi-parameter erosion of American primacy: (1) Military -- Cathay closes conventional gap within 3 years, already leads in shipbuilding 200:1; (2) Economic -- Cathay GDP approaching parity in PPP, dominates manufacturing, rare earths, batteries; (3) Technology -- Cathay close behind on AI, ahead on 5G and quantum; (4) Alliances -- allies diversifying relationships with Cathay, EU pursuing autonomy, Gulf states hedging; (5) Financial -- BRICS+ currency alternatives threaten dollar monopoly on which sanctions power rests. The window to act from strength is closing. But acting too aggressively accelerates the very fractures it aims to prevent.

key_relationships.cathay: strategic competitor -- contain through tariffs and tech restrictions, avoid direct conflict
key_relationships.nordostan: adversary -- sanction and isolate but avoid direct conflict
key_relationships.heartland: ally -- support with arms and money but not troops
key_relationships.gallia: ally -- coordinate within NATO but manage EU autonomy aspirations
key_relationships.teutonia: ally -- encourage defense spending, worry about China trade dependency
key_relationships.yamato: key ally -- Pacific anchor, support remilitarization
key_relationships.formosa: protectorate -- defend if attacked but prefer strategic ambiguity
key_relationships.bharata: court aggressively -- need on sanctions coalition, offer tech deals
key_relationships.levantia: ally -- restrain from unilateral Persia strikes, share intelligence
key_relationships.persia: adversary -- prevent nuclear weapons through sanctions and threat
key_relationships.albion: closest ally -- Five Eyes, intelligence sharing, coordinate everything
key_relationships.hanguk: ally -- maintain troop presence, tech cooperation
key_relationships.solaria: transactional partner -- need oil stability, overlook human rights

red_lines: Nuclear weapons use by any party

profiles.primary_goal: Close the power gap with Columbia within the next 3-4 years. Chairman (age 72) sees this as his legacy -- particularly resolving the Formosa question. China's naval buildup is on track to overtake the US Pacific fleet by 2028-2029. The window is opening but will close as Columbia potentially reaches AI L4 breakthrough. Every year of patience is a year closer to military parity -- but also a year the Chairman ages without his legacy secured.
secondary_goals: Build naval superiority in Western Pacific
Achieve AI Level 3 before Columbia reaches Level 4
Reduce dependency on Formosa semiconductors
Expand influence through Belt and Road and BRICS+
ticking_clocks: Naval parity with Columbia by 2028-2029
Chairman age 72, no successor, legacy = Formosa
AI tech race -- if Columbia reaches L4 first, window closes permanently
Economic slowdown reduces the growth advantage that funds the buildup
profiles.strategic_analysis: Multi-parameter assessment: Military gap closing (favorable). Nuclear approaching L3 (favorable). AI tech -- behind Columbia but closing (uncertain). Economic growth slowing from 6% to 4% (concerning). Alliance architecture -- BRICS+ growing but lacks NATO cohesion (mixed). Formosa semiconductor leverage declines as alternatives emerge (time-limited). Overall: the next 3 years are the optimal window. Delay risks permanent disadvantage if Columbia achieves AI L4.

key_relationships.columbia: strategic rival -- avoid open confrontation while building strength, exploit domestic divisions
key_relationships.nordostan: junior partner -- useful distraction for Columbia, supply with tech, buy energy
key_relationships.formosa: renegade province -- reunification is personal legacy, prefer peaceful coercion
key_relationships.yamato: historical adversary -- contain through economic leverage, watch remilitarization
key_relationships.bharata: competitive partner -- border tension but BRICS+ alignment
key_relationships.hanguk: economic dependency target -- use trade leverage to weaken Columbia alliance
key_relationships.solaria: energy supplier -- diversify away from Hormuz vulnerability
key_relationships.persia: strategic partner -- energy and anti-Columbia alignment
key_relationships.choson: buffer state -- restrain from provocations that invite Columbia presence
key_relationships.teutonia: trade partner -- exploit dependency to prevent sanctions alignment

red_lines: Columbia permanent military base on Formosa
escalation_triggers: Columbia arms sale to Formosa above threshold
Columbia Pacific fleet surge near Taiwan Strait
Internal economic crisis requiring nationalist distraction

profiles.primary_goal: Produce something that can be called 'victory' in the Heartland war before resources run out. The President (age 73) has no safe exit -- personal survival depends on maintaining power, and power depends on the war producing results. The economy has proven surprisingly resilient under sanctions (4 years and counting) but is slowly eroding. The military is degraded but functional. Nuclear weapons remain the ultimate insurance. The question is not whether to fight, but what terms of settlement preserve the regime.
secondary_goals: Prevent NATO expansion to Heartland
Maintain nuclear deterrent as ultimate guarantee
Keep oil revenue flowing despite sanctions
Prevent regime collapse from war exhaustion
ticking_clocks: Economic erosion -- sanctions slowly bite despite resilience
Military attrition -- losing experienced troops faster than replacing them
War fatigue -- society tolerates the war but enthusiasm is gone
China's junior partner -- the longer the war, the more dependent on Cathay
Leader age 73, no successor -- personal survival = regime survival
profiles.strategic_analysis: Resilience, not collapse: the economy adapted to sanctions through import substitution, China trade, and oil revenue. GDP contracted 2% initially, now growing again at 1-2%. But the military industrial base is strained, and the skilled workforce is depleting. Nuclear capability remains the regime's insurance -- no external power will risk forcing regime change on a nuclear-armed state. The goal is a settlement that preserves territorial gains and regime dignity. Escalation (nuclear threats, energy weaponization) is a tool for negotiation, not warfare.

key_relationships.columbia: primary adversary -- nuclear parity keeps them at bay
key_relationships.heartland: existential enemy -- must be neutralized or partitioned
key_relationships.cathay: senior partner -- need their economy and UN veto
key_relationships.persia: partner -- coordinate sanctions evasion, arms sales

key_relationships.solaria: OPEC+ partner -- coordinate production for revenue
key_relationships.bharata: arms customer -- maintain relationship for UN votes

secondary_goals: Maintain Western military aid pipeline
Keep domestic morale despite war exhaustion
Prepare for wartime election (Round 3-4)
Build defensive capacity faster than Nordostan can attack

key_relationships.columbia: essential patron -- survival depends on continued aid, fear of aid fatigue
key_relationships.nordostan: existential enemy -- no compromise on sovereignty

profiles.primary_goal: Achieve nuclear threshold capability without triggering preemptive strike. Maintain regime survival through nuclear ambiguity.
secondary_goals: Expand proxy network influence in Middle East
Reduce sanctions pressure through diplomatic openings
Control Strait of Hormuz as leverage
Balance hardliner, reformist, and military factions internally
ticking_clocks: Nuclear threshold approaching -- Levantia will strike if progress exceeds 80%
Proxy network degraded by Levantia operations -- rebuilding takes time
Internal reform pressure -- younger population demands economic opening
IRGC economic empire -- military-industrial faction has veto power over diplomacy
profiles.strategic_analysis: The regime operates on multiple tracks simultaneously. Track 1: Nuclear ambiguity -- stay close enough to a weapon to deter attack, far enough to avoid triggering one. Track 2: Proxy network as regional insurance -- even if bombed, proxies continue the fight. Track 3: Hormuz leverage -- the ability to shut down 20% of global oil gives disproportionate negotiating power relative to GDP. Track 4: Great power relationships -- Cathay buys oil regardless of Western sanctions, Nordostan sells weapons and provides UN cover. The internal debate: hardliners want to sprint to a weapon, IRGC pragmatists want threshold status for leverage, reformists want to trade nuclear ambiguity for sanctions relief. The Supreme Leader balances all three, tilting toward threshold status.

key_relationships.columbia: primary adversary -- sanctions source, military threat

escalation_triggers: Columbia carrier group in Persian Gulf
Nuclear deal collapse with no diplomatic exit

profiles.primary_goal: Champion EU strategic autonomy. Lead Europe as independent power pole, not American vassal.
secondary_goals: Maintain nuclear deterrent credibility
Build European defense capability
Protect French industry from Chinese competition
Mediate conflicts to enhance diplomatic prestige
profiles.strategic_analysis: Gallia occupies a unique position: nuclear-armed, UNSC permanent member, and the only EU state with genuine power projection capability. In the US-China competition, Gallia sees opportunity for Europe as a third pole -- not equidistant, but autonomous. The Nordostan-Heartland war validates European defense investment but also reveals painful dependence on Columbia for heavy logistics and intelligence. Gallia's calculation: support Heartland enough to maintain credibility, push EU defense integration to reduce Columbia dependency, maintain Cathay trade to avoid economic damage, and position Paris as the indispensable mediator. Risk: if Columbia demands loyalty tests (anti-Cathay sanctions, force commitments), Gallia must choose between alliance and autonomy.

key_relationships.columbia: ally but rival -- resist American dominance of alliance, maintain independence
key_relationships.teutonia: essential partner -- Franco-German axis drives EU, frustration with pacifism
key_relationships.albion: post-Brexit competitor -- cooperate on defense but compete on influence
key_relationships.nordostan: adversary -- support Heartland but keep diplomatic channel open
key_relationships.cathay: economic partner -- resist Columbia pressure for full decoupling
key_relationships.heartland: supported nation -- military aid within budget limits
key_relationships.bharata: strategic partner -- defense sales, Indo-Pacific presence
key_relationships.levantia: complicated -- historical ties, tension over settlements

red_lines: American unilateralism that damages EU interests
escalation_triggers: Columbia demanding anti-China sanctions that hurt EU

profiles.primary_goal: Preserve European stability and economic prosperity. Avoid being forced to choose between Columbia and Cathay.
secondary_goals: Rebuild military capability (Zeitenwende) without bankrupting social state
Maintain Cathay trade relationship despite alliance pressure
Support Heartland within politically sustainable limits
Lead EU economic policy coordination
profiles.strategic_analysis: Teutonia is the most exposed European power in the US-China competition. Its economic model depends on exporting to Cathay (largest trade partner), importing energy (now diversified away from Nordostan but still vulnerable), and sheltering under Columbia's security umbrella. The Zeitenwende defense buildup is real but slow -- military spending increased but the Bundeswehr remains years from credible deterrence. In the Nordostan-Heartland war, Teutonia is the largest European donor but politically terrified of escalation. The nightmare scenario: forced to choose between Cathay trade (economic survival) and Columbia alliance (security survival). Strategy: delay that choice as long as possible through diplomatic ambiguity and incremental concessions to both sides.

key_relationships.columbia: essential protector -- but resent pressure on China trade
key_relationships.cathay: critical trade partner -- largest export market, cannot afford full decoupling
key_relationships.gallia: EU partner -- sometimes clashing on defense spending and industrial policy
key_relationships.nordostan: former partner turned adversary -- energy dependency broken, still fear escalation
key_relationships.heartland: supported nation -- generous aid but terrified of escalation
key_relationships.freeland: eastern ally -- respect their security concerns, coordinate NATO eastern flank

profiles.primary_goal: Maximum security against Nordostan threat. Become the eastern pillar of NATO.
secondary_goals: Build the strongest military in continental EU
Host more Columbia military assets
Support Heartland unconditionally
Lead NATO eastern flank coordination
profiles.strategic_analysis: Freeland sees the Nordostan-Heartland war as an existential preview: if Heartland falls, Freeland is next. This drives the most aggressive military buildup in Europe -- defense spending at 4% of GDP and rising. Freeland's position in the US-China competition is simple: whatever keeps Columbia engaged in European security. Unlike Gallia or Teutonia, Freeland has no interest in strategic autonomy -- it wants MORE American troops, not fewer. Freeland lobbies relentlessly within NATO for maximum defense posture, frustrating allies who prefer diplomacy. The risk: Freeland's hawkish stance could split NATO consensus, ironically weakening the alliance it depends on.

key_relationships.columbia: essential protector -- want permanent bases and more troops
key_relationships.nordostan: existential threat -- only language they understand is force
key_relationships.heartland: brother nation -- if Heartland falls, Freeland is next
key_relationships.teutonia: frustrating ally -- too slow on defense, too soft on Nordostan
key_relationships.gallia: ally -- appreciate nuclear umbrella but dislike EU autonomy talk

profiles.primary_goal: Manage massive debt burden while maintaining political stability. Extract maximum benefit from EU/NATO membership.
secondary_goals: Avoid debt crisis triggering EU fiscal intervention
Play swing voter role in EU/NATO for concessions
Maintain Mediterranean naval presence
Balance domestic populism with alliance obligations
profiles.strategic_analysis: Ponte is the weakest link in the Western alliance chain. Debt at 140% of GDP makes every policy choice a fiscal calculation. In the US-China competition, Ponte is tempted by Cathay infrastructure investment (Belt and Road) but constrained by alliance obligations. In the Nordostan-Heartland war, sanctions compliance hurts the Italian economy through higher energy costs. Ponte's strategy: use its swing-vote position in EU and NATO to extract concessions -- fiscal flexibility, infrastructure funds, migration support -- in exchange for loyalty. The risk: if domestic populists gain power, Ponte could veto EU sanctions or invite Cathay investment that compromises alliance security.

key_relationships.columbia: distant ally -- want attention but often overlooked
key_relationships.gallia: EU partner and rival -- compete for Mediterranean influence
key_relationships.nordostan: reluctant adversary -- sanctions hurt, energy costs painful
key_relationships.cathay: tempting investor -- Belt and Road offers infrastructure money
key_relationships.phrygia: Mediterranean neighbor -- migration and Libya cooperation

red_lines: Debt crisis and EU bailout conditions

profiles.primary_goal: Project relevance as global power despite post-Brexit constraints. Leverage intelligence advantage and nuclear status.
secondary_goals: Maintain special relationship with Columbia
Lead on intelligence and cyber capabilities
Build Indo-Pacific tilt as strategic differentiator
Support Heartland as demonstration of global relevance
profiles.strategic_analysis: Post-Brexit Albion seeks relevance through two pillars: the Five Eyes intelligence advantage (unmatched in Europe) and nuclear deterrent credibility. In the US-China competition, Albion has chosen sides more clearly than any European ally -- AUKUS, Indo-Pacific tilt, Huawei ban. In the Nordostan-Heartland war, Albion leads on intelligence sharing and sanctions enforcement, punching above its weight. The strategy: be Columbia's indispensable partner in every theater, making the special relationship irreplaceable. Risk: overstretch. A small economy and shrinking military cannot credibly operate in Europe, the Indo-Pacific, and the Middle East simultaneously. Scotland independence pressure adds domestic fragility.

key_relationships.columbia: special relationship -- intelligence sharing, military coordination, follow lead
key_relationships.gallia: rival and partner -- post-Brexit tension but defense cooperation
key_relationships.nordostan: adversary -- lead on sanctions enforcement and intelligence
key_relationships.bharata: historical connection -- trade and defense deals

escalation_triggers: Intelligence indicating imminent attack on ally

profiles.primary_goal: Maximize strategic autonomy. Extract maximum concessions from both Columbia and Cathay blocs without committing to either.
secondary_goals: Sustain 6%+ GDP growth as domestic legitimacy foundation
Build indigenous defense and tech capability
Secure energy imports at favorable prices (buy Russian oil at discount)
Manage border tension with Cathay
ticking_clocks: Demographic dividend window -- largest young workforce in history, but only for 15-20 years
Cathay border tension -- unresolved Ladakh standoff could escalate
Tech ambitions -- must build indigenous semiconductor and AI capacity before supply chains lock in
Energy transition -- massive oil import dependency creates vulnerability to price shocks
profiles.strategic_analysis: Bharata has perfected multi-alignment diplomacy: buying discounted Nordostan oil while deepening Columbia tech partnerships; joining BRICS+ while participating in the Quad; abstaining on UN votes while accepting defense technology from all sides. This works because BOTH blocs want Bharata more than Bharata needs either. Columbia needs Bharata for the democratic alliance narrative and China containment; Cathay needs Bharata for BRICS+ legitimacy and to prevent full encirclement. Bharata's leverage: 1.4 billion consumers, the world's fastest-growing major economy, and a swing vote in every international institution. The strategy: extract maximum concessions from the highest bidder on each issue, commit to neither, build indigenous capability so the leverage grows over time. Risk: a Formosa crisis or Cathay border escalation could force the choice Bharata desperately wants to avoid.

key_relationships.columbia: important partner -- tech deals and defense cooperation, but resist pressure to join anti-Cathay bloc
key_relationships.cathay: rival and neighbor -- border disputes, competition for regional influence, trade partner

profiles.primary_goal: Prevent Persia from acquiring nuclear weapons. Maintain regional military dominance.
secondary_goals: Degrade Persia's proxy network
Maintain qualitative military edge over all regional rivals
Strengthen Columbia alliance and intelligence sharing
Manage domestic coalition politics
ticking_clocks: Persia nuclear progress -- once they cross the threshold, conventional deterrence fails
Proxy network reconstitution -- degraded networks rebuild within 2-3 years
Columbia attention span -- re-election politics may shift American focus away from Middle East
Regional normalization momentum -- Abraham Accords create incentives for restraint but also vulnerability
profiles.strategic_analysis: Levantia faces a classic security dilemma: preemptive action against Persia nuclear sites would set back the program 3-5 years but trigger massive retaliation through proxies and Hormuz disruption. Inaction risks a nuclear-armed Persia that is permanently undeterrable. The calculation: strike probability increases nonlinearly as Persia approaches the threshold. Below 60% progress: diplomacy and sabotage. Between 60-80%: intense covert operations. Above 80%: military strike becomes likely. Columbia's support is essential -- Levantia needs American bunker-buster munitions and diplomatic cover. The ongoing proxy degradation campaign buys time but is not a substitute for addressing the nuclear program directly.
